speaker: interviewer
text: I'm reading it? Oh, today is August 29, 1995. The survivor we're doing the interview with is Rachel Schlufman. Maiden name is Warszauer. The interviewer is Shelley Schore. We're in Brooklyn, New York in the United States. And the interview will be in English. My name is Shelley Schore. I'm interviewing Rachel Warszauer Schlufman. Today is August 29, 1995. We're in Brooklyn, New York in the United States. And we're interviewing in English. OK, please say your name for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Rachel Schlufman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell it-- the last name please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Schlufman, S-C-H-L-U-F-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name that you were born with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Warszauer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell it please.

speaker: interviewee
text: W-A-R-S-Z-A-U-E-R, Warszauer.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your date of birth, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the town where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wachock.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please spell that.

speaker: interviewee
text: W-A-C-H-O-C-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's in country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wachock was situated in Poland-- middle Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Describe the town for me a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: The town was a small town, a little shtetl. But that little shtetl had everything. We were about over a hundred Jewish families, about over 500 Christian families. We lived relatively nicely together. In my time-- in my-- in my time, those years, I did not count no anti-Semitism. I didn't know too much about it. I was too young. The Jewish people, mostly-- they were-- there were no professionals in my little town. There were shoe makers, you know, [NON-ENGLISH], dressmakers, all kind of-- and mostly business. Because our city, we weren't too far away from an ammunition factory from [INAUDIBLE]. So most of the Christian people, they bought from the Jewish people, you know, groceries, and hardware, and textile. The people, most of the people, were-- they had stores around.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, and they did-- they did very well-- all these people. Because they were a small population of Jewish people. And the most population was the Poles. So relatively they did very nicely, you know? They got along together?

speaker: interviewee
text: They got along. I remember as a child when I went to school I didn't have special Christian [NON-ENGLISH] friends. Because we Jewish people lived all together. We had a nice synagogue in the city. We had a library. We had a cemetery. We had a train, a subway station, a train going-- taking us wherever we wanted to go, like the Warsaw, and Radom, and many cities around, which was very convenient for us. People who wanted to-- young people who wanted to study, they could take the train and go to another city and come back in the evening. We didn't have-- we only had elementary school. And we had a Hebrew school. We had all the convenience in our shtetl, all the convenience I will say.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of elementary school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a public school. It was also half and half-- elementary, I would say, and Catholic. The priest will come in. We, Jewish students, would walk out while he was teaching the Christian kids you know, The Bible. We respect them. He would take them twice a week. And he will teach one hour. Then we will come back like nothing. And that was respect. In the winter time, I remember, it was too cold to go out in the backyard, the school backyard. So we would stood up and respect them. And I would listen, like make believe I don't listen, you know. But that was a part we had to attend that Catholic school. Because Poland is mostly, in the smaller cities, Catholic schools. In the big cities, the Jewish people could attend private schools, Jewish schools, universities. But we didn't have that. But it was a must. Everybody had to go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of Hebrew school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Hebrew school which was called [NON-ENGLISH]. They were only for girls. We learned the Hebrew history. We learned the history, the folk-dancing for two hours every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was after you--

speaker: interviewee
text: After we came home 12 o'clock from school, we ate lunch, and I prepared my work to go to Hebrew school. 3 o'clock, I came back. I did my homework from my public school, then for my Hebrew school. And then the evening fell. And it was a custom to be home, you know, not to play around late in the evening. And that's the life in the shtetl, you know. I used to help my mother. In Europe, it wasn't like-- every child had to give a hand to her mother. We were-- I had-- we were five brothers and three sisters. Of course, two sisters were married. When I was with my parents, I was a little girl when they got out from house. And, you know, I used to help my mother here and there a little bit with housework or certain little things. My favorite thing was, like, shining my fathers shoes or doing things around in the house straighten out. And my mother would send me out. Get me this or get me that from the grocery, little things. Life-- life was pleasant in those years. It was very good in the shtetl. The most thing it was the home, which I loved my home and my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: My friends was-- they were everything to me. They used to come to my house. I used to go to their house. We used to play as children. And it-- youth is beautiful. But wasn't too long for me. It was very, very short. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a little girl, I remember very little. And a couple years before the war, it was very short. So it had turned very quickly, really. But as much as I remember, I cherish everything of it. And I love everything whatever I remember, you know, special the love from my parents. We never used to quarrel at home. Our brothers were brought up very fine, educated. And as I got older, before the war broke out, my brothers attended the yeshiva. They weren't home. My two brothers, like, Zisman and Leibl, Leibl went to Yeshiva of Lublin. My father used to be very, very proud of him. He was really an outstanding student and besides good looking, kind, such a fine fellow. And my parents took pride of her sons, when she saw them going to shul Friday night with my father. She took a lot of pride [SOBBING] when they walked out. To her everyone individual looked so handsome and good. I still remember her looks when she watched them walking out going to shul with my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course, my parents, my brothers-- they were very orthodox. And the whole-- my family very orthodox-- not just my family, my grandparents, uncles, and aunts. The whole family, all from cousins to aunts, they were orthodox people. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And most of the-- it went from, like, from generation to generation to send the kids to yeshivas and to learn. And I, myself-- I, myself, wanted to be a Hebrew teacher. I loved my school. And my teacher always complimented me. I took very-- I took a lot of pride learning the Jewish history and about Israel. I always dreamed when I was a kid about Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: I loved the [INAUDIBLE] Israeli dancing when she was teaching, our teacher. And-- and I dreamed at night that I would like sometimes the life in Israel and go sometimes to Israel. You know? I would never think that 50 years would pass by, you know, that Israel would be so close to me and so near that I could go any time in my life. You know? Because those years, it was just a dream that we would go to Israel. You know, it was just like a pray. Tell me your father's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Szlomo, Szlomo. I-- we called him-- his name was-- I don't remember he had a second name. [? Schloima, ?] we called him [? Schloima. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a-- in America, I will say ritual slaughterer. But I will explain to you what a ritual slaughterer really means. A ritual slaughterer means in Hebrew, we say [SPEAKING HEBREW]. [SPEAKING HEBREW] means the man has to have the same education like a rabbi. He calls himself a rabbi, but he has a trait which he becomes a [SPEAKING HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: What is a [SPEAKING HEBREW]? The [SPEAKING HEBREW] is he has to have his skill, his education-- how to-- to-- kill that-- the cow, to kill the animal with such a fast movement that the cow, the animal, shouldn't feel no pain. This goes back about in the-- now, in religious time, about 2,000 years ago when the [NON-ENGLISH] made that law. I really didn't read the Bible for where it comes from.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I remember as the little girl how my father used to sharpen the knife. It was a long narrow knife, very long and narrow. And he used to sharpen that knife for hours. And he used to check that knife on his nail how sharp that knife was. I used to get sometimes dizzy to look how my father used to, you know, test it. And sometimes I feared that he shouldn't cut his nail to see how sharp. When it was sharp enough, then he stopped it. And he had a few knives, a special knife for the chicken, a special knife for a-- for a duck, for-- for-- for goose, you know. And you know, I remember all this. But he had to be learned and have the skills and be very educated in that-- that-- his profession. And then after he slaughtered, he had to examine if the animal was in a good condition. If the animal had any disease, my father had to be so educated to know if the lungs were, you know, weren't working or that the heart wasn't good or something was there not right inside. He would say to the butcher this animal is not kosher. And it was very difficult. Because during the war, it was very difficult for a [SPEAKING HEBREW]. Because half of the part was going to the Christians. And that part was going to the Jewish people for kosher food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the butcher didn't know what to do with the other part when it wasn't allowed during the war, you know, to deal with the Christian. So it was very, very difficult-- very difficult for a butcher to deal and to get that animal. Before the Holocaust, the-- the meat that your father decided was not kosher-- what happened to it? It went to the non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I tell you. I will explain you what had happened. The other part, it was-- the butcher was selling the other part to the non-Jewish people. They went to the butcher, but they know this meat. It was in a different section that belongs-- you know, it was kosher. But it wasn't kosher. Because why it wasn't kosher really-- the veins go through that meat. And this has to be taken out. But an animal has little, little veins like a human. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's very difficult from the bottom part to take all these little veins out. It will take days. And kosher meat, it's only-- after three days, it's not kosher. You know? It has to be kosher. So mostly, the butcher sold it to the Christians, which they did a good business, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, we had very few Christian butchers. The only butchers the war, it was Jewish. And the Christians, they like to buy that part of the meat, which was very good meat-- the-- the lower part. So it turned out that a [NON-ENGLISH] was never rich. He couldn't become rich. But he was a Talmudical scholar. He was a rabbi. He was a very-- he was very fine and educated man, which people in the city respect him. As, like, you know, different. You know, he was very educated in his-- in his law. Actually a [NON-ENGLISH] is like a veterinarian-- veterinarian's law, the same education. You know, you have to study when an animal can live. When the animal has no-- cannot live without disease, the animal is not kosher. And that's very important. So for my father to study before he became a [NON-ENGLISH], it's like you study like doctor, you know, to be a doctor in that profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even a chicken-- sometimes, if you open a chicken in those years, sometimes we did see a nail going through the stomach. It wasn't kosher. The chicken could not live with a rusted nail going through, you know? So many times it was so difficult.

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't so-- it was-- it was funny, but was not funny when many times a woman would come in with a chicken, you know, covered with a white tissue or towel. And she will say to my father please, Mr. Warszauer. Would you-- you know, I have the chicken. And it's Friday late.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell me? I found this and this. Would you tell me if the chicken is kosher? She was standing so shivering and praying that chicken that my father will come out from his [NON-ENGLISH]-- that means from the other room-- and he will say the chicken is kosher. What will she do when she goes home with unkosher chicken? What will she give her kids to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I looked at the woman, and my heart was beating many times. And I said, please, God. That chicken should be kosher. And I was a little girl. My father should only pronounce to the woman that chicken is kosher. Otherwise, I could not face that woman. And my father used to come out with a smile, I knew the chicken is kosher. He says, it's kosher. Oh, thank God. The chicken is kosher. She blessed my father. And she ran home so fast, of course, to cook the chicken for her family. But many times my mother will open the chicken and she will say to my father, [? Shlomie. ?] I find something in the chicken [NON-ENGLISH] chicken [NON-ENGLISH]. My father says, wait a minute, wait a minute. Let me see it. He walked over, and he looks at the chicken. And he says, it's [NON-ENGLISH]. It's on the other side. It's kosher, don't worry. So my mother was happy. She gave my father a smile. Oh, she was so happy. Then she started to work on the chicken-- to pluck-- you know, pluck the feathers, to make it kosher, and so, and so. Hard work went on in that-- at home and in every home in our shtetl. In many shtetl around Poland, that was the life. That was the basic life of Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me your mother's name. We-- we talked about your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Beila.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Teitelbaum.

speaker: interviewee
text: Teitelbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you describe to me a little bit what it was like before Shabbat in your house--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before Shabbat--

speaker: interviewer
text: --what your mother prepared?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will tell you. Before Shabbat was a big, big job at home, especially in our home. As we were all orthodox, we all took pride on Shabbas. Sabbath was the most wonderful day that we could enjoy-- all day long, all week we went to school. We hustled. My father worked. My mother was busy crocheting, and cooking, and washing, and mending, and this, and this, you know, cleaning the clothes with benzene those years. We didn't have cleaning stores. Everything had to be made by hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: So preparing for that Sabbath-- the cleaning my father's hat-- velvet hat and cleaning his-- his wardrobe and this and that, and preparing that velvet yarmulke you had to clean with that benzene. You know? And it took place on a Thursday. We prepared the shopping on a Thursday. We had a-- a market. What do you call it? [NON-ENGLISH]. This was every Thursday.

speaker: interviewee
text: The [NON-ENGLISH] lasted for a few hours, from morning to 1 o'clock. Most of the people, most of these handlers, they came from the farms. They brought eggs, chickens, live fish, sour pickles, apples, onions, [NON-ENGLISH], potatoes, carrots, whatever. It was everything you could buy. The only thing you needed is money in any place.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland before the war, there wasn't such a-- you know, you could buy everything. You could buy chocolate, and halvah, and oranges from everything, whatever you want. But the only thing that you needed is money. The people who had money lived beautiful, lived relatively the same like in America, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And for us, it was a little difficult. My father-- you know, it was very difficult for us to-- because we were a household of-- without my sisters, they were married-- they [INAUDIBLE] six of us ate together. It was-- you know, my mother had to go out and shop. She bought whatever she could. But she was very happy that she could provide and to buy the necessity what she could provide for that Sabbath. All week long she didn't care. But Sabbath was the most important-- that to observe and conduct the Sabbath, and cook-- you know, to cook and have all the food. Not every time, but she tried. They both tried-- my parents tried with all their heart and soul to provide for that Sabbath.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I also I wanted you to tell me a little bit about on the Sabbath your responsibility with taking the cholent to the Baker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was my responsibility, because I was the only girl surrounded with brothers. Usually, boys didn't do that job. It was indecent. It was a shame, especially for orthodox boys with pay especially. They wouldn't go for that. They didn't go shopping, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had the responsibility to take that cholent to the baker and bring it back on Saturday about 11 o'clock, you know, when we were ready to eat the cholent. Before 12:00, usually, they came home from shul. Many-- For those people who don't know what cholent is, explain it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cholent-- Cholent means that-- we-- we used to have the cholent in the summer. Why? Because the house was very hot. And to make a fire in the stove, this would be too hot. So most of the people in our city, they made cholent. A cholent means you can make barley with beans. Or, you make-- most-- my mother used to make potatoes with a piece of meat, you know, with a little [NON-ENGLISH] she used to make it. You know, fill that [NON-ENGLISH] with flour. And she used to put it in between the potatoes, wrap around with paper all around with the-- and to protect it, you know, to protect that the steam shouldn't come out. And-- and I would take the cholent to the butcher.

speaker: interviewer
text: The baker.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the baker. But I, many times when I was too young, I always was so nervous if I would remember the cholent. Because sometimes the paper got brown. And I wouldn't remember what cholent, how the pot looked. And I used to get so nervous that many times I didn't sleep at night. When I-- when I brought the cholent-- the right cholent home, my mother used to bless me. Oh, my [NON-ENGLISH]. And when she uncovered and when saw the cholent is-- is brown and came out good, she was so happy that she will be able to feed her family with a good cholent which she made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was-- that was the most wonderful dish in our home in those years. And winter was easier for me. Why was it so easy for me? I used to look forward for the winter. Because in the winter, we had a Polish man. He was going around from house the house and kindle-- the-- the light in the wood what he-- we were-- he burned in the stove.

speaker: interviewee
text: The man was a skinny man, an elderly man looking poor. His hand was burned from the ashes. But he was doing weekly routinely, week after week. I used to feel sorry for him. He knocked on the door. I was so happy. I jumped down from the bed and said, ah, here he is. He took out the match. And he lightened up. And he stood, and he stood. He waited a few minutes. The light didn't go on. And I says, oh, God, please. Let that fire start, because the house was so cold. I could see the design from the ice on the window. We couldn't look out. In Poland, if the cold was so-- for three months-- for three months, the snow was laying there on the ground. It was very cold. We didn't have no heat. This was the only pleasure. What we have is the oven warmed our hearts. And we also had an oven in the main room. But in the big-- in the master room, the oven, we didn't burn it on the-- on the Saturday. We just put wood their and coals Friday night. And then it burned little by little a whole night. So it stayed a little of warm. But, of course, all around was warm. When you walked away, the house was cold. But the kitchen was the main thing. We sat in the kitchen. And many times that fire didn't go out. And he walked out. And I looked in and I said, no fire. Oh, God. I went back to the bed, and I covered myself. I didn't want to bring the news to my mother. Because she will get furious. Oh, I didn't watch him. Because I would-- I would be the one. But I slept in the kitchen. I should've watched that man to make sure that the fire is burning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told my mother, it's cold. The fire is not burning. She says you better get dressed and go out and look for him. He must be in somebody else's house. Bring him back. What are we going to do? But many times the snow was piled up. I didn't have no boots to go out. I went back to bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, I don't want to eat. I will starve myself a whole day. But I'm not going out in the snow and look for this old man to come. He should come back and make that fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking back those things how it was-- when I look back, were they nice? Were they good? Was it fun? A different life. It was so much different. It's hard to understand for my sons, to tell them. I would-- I never told them that life, how it was. Because they will think it would be so weird. Why wouldn't I open that little door from the oven and make just a few blows that fire should start? Why was I so fanatic? You know? I would be afraid to open that little door. I would have sinned. I wasn't allowed to do it on Shabbas, on Sabbath-- the biggest sin. I would think as a child that God will punish me, and someday I'm going to die. So who would take a chance as a child open that little door from that oven and blow? So I never-- it never came across that I should tell my sons those things really.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when we come together, survivors, we always talk about it. Sometimes we laugh, you know, from all those things. But it's nice to bring back these memories how life was then in our--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
